Red Swastika School — P6 Science WA2 2024

Primary 6 Science · Weighted Assessment 2 (Class Test 2) · 2024
Section A: Multiple-Choice Questions (9 × 2 = 18 marks) · Section B: Open-Ended Questions (12 marks) · 45 minutes

Section A: Multiple-Choice Questions

Question 1

2 marks

The diagram below shows the location of parent plants, A and B, and their young.

Key
solid triangle (▲)parent plant A
open triangle (△)young of plant A
solid square (■)parent plant B
open square (□)young of plant B
arrowswater direction & wind direction

Which of the following best represents the method of dispersal of plants A and B? (Each option shows a seed of Plant A and a seed of Plant B.)

Answer

Option 1. The young of plant A (open triangles) are spread out on the same side of the river as parent A, in the direction of the wind — so A is dispersed by wind, which matches a seed with a wing-like structure. The young of plant B (open squares) are clustered close to parent B on the far side — B is dispersed by splitting/explosive action, matching dry pods. Options 2–4 give a fibrous husk (water dispersal) or hooks (animal dispersal), which do not fit the wind-and-pod pattern shown.

Question 2

2 marks

The table below shows the freezing and boiling points of substances A, B and C.

SubstanceFreezing point (°C)Boiling point (°C)
A4055
B3580
C70110

Which of the substance(s) is/are likely to be liquid at 60°C?

  1. B only
  2. C only
  3. A and B only
  4. A and C only

Answer

Option 1. A substance is liquid between its freezing point and boiling point. At 60°C: A (40–55°C) is above its boiling point, so it is a gas; B (35–80°C) is between, so it is a liquid; C freezes at 70°C, so at 60°C it is still a solid. Only B is liquid.

Question 3

2 marks

The diagram below shows the movement of blood in the human circulatory system.

Which of the following is true about the blood in X and Y?

XY
(1)High in carbon dioxideLow in oxygen
(2)Low in carbon dioxideHigh in oxygen
(3)High in oxygenHigh in carbon dioxide
(4)Low in oxygenLow in carbon dioxide

Answer

Option 3. X carries blood leaving the lungs to the heart — it has just been oxygenated, so it is high in oxygen. Y carries blood returning from all other parts of the body to the heart — the body cells have used the oxygen and released carbon dioxide, so it is high in carbon dioxide. Option 3 matches both.

Question 4

2 marks

Samantha placed a plant with white flowers into a beaker of red-coloured water. After a few days she cut a cross-section of the stem and made her observations.

What would Samantha observe about the plant with white flowers?

  1. Only the flowers turned red.
  2. The water-carrying tubes and white flowers turned red.
  3. The food-carrying tubes and the white flowers turned red.
  4. The water-carrying tubes and the food-carrying tubes turned red.

Answer

Option 2. The red-coloured water is absorbed by the roots and transported up the stem through the water-carrying tubes (xylem), staining them red. The water reaches the petals, turning the white flowers red. The food-carrying tubes (phloem) do not transport water from the roots, so they are not stained.

Question 5

2 marks

Dan observed three different cells, X, Y and Z, under a microscope and recorded his observations.

XYZ
cell membrane✓✓✓
nucleus✓✓✓
chloroplast✓
cell wall✓✓

He then made the following statements:

A: Only X is able to make food.
B: X and Z are both plant cells.
C: Y and Z both have fixed shapes.

Which of the statement(s) is/are correct?

  1. A only
  2. C only
  3. A and B only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 3. Statement A is correct: only X has chloroplasts, so only X can make food by photosynthesis. Statement B is correct: X and Z both have a cell wall, so both are plant cells. Statement C is wrong: Y has no cell wall, so it does not have a fixed shape (only Z, with a cell wall, has a fixed shape). So A and B only are correct.

Question 6

2 marks

The diagram below shows an electric circuit.

Which of the following observations is correct?

  1. When all the switches are closed, all the bulbs will light up.
  2. When S2 and S3 are closed, only bulbs C and D will light up.
  3. When S1, S3 and S4 are closed, none of the bulbs will light up.
  4. When S1, S2 and S4 are closed, bulbs A, C and D will light up.

Answer

Option 3. The battery is in the bottom branch (bulbs C and D). The wooden rod is an insulator, so the top branch (bulb A) is always broken. With S1, S3 and S4 closed but S2 open, the current leaving the battery can only return through bulb B and the metal rod to the middle-left junction, but from there S2 is open and the only other way out (through S1) leads into the dead-end wooden-rod branch. No complete circuit can be formed, so none of the bulbs light up.

Question 7

2 marks

The diagram below shows a pond habitat.

Which of the following statements is correct?

  1. There are 3 organisms.
  2. There are 6 populations of fish.
  3. There is 1 population of producer.
  4. There are 2 populations of consumers.

Answer

Option 4. A population is all the organisms of the same kind in a habitat. The consumers shown are the frog (the tadpoles are young frogs, so they belong to the same population) and the fish — that is 2 populations of consumers. Option 3 is wrong because there are two kinds of producer (the lily pad and the underwater water plant), not one. The fish form a single population (not 6), and there are far more than 3 individual organisms.

Question 8

2 marks

Study the food web below.

Which of the following statements are true?

A: M is a producer.
B: O is a plant-and-animal eater.
C: R preys on O and N.
D: When P decreases, the population of S will decrease.

  1. A and B
  2. C and D
  3. A, B and D
  4. A, C and D

Answer

Option 3. A is true: M has only arrows leaving it and none entering, so it is the producer. B is true: O receives arrows from M (a plant) and from N (an animal), so O eats both plants and animals — it is a plant-and-animal eater. D is true: S is fed only by P (P→S), so if P decreases, S decreases. C is false: the arrows into R come from P and O, so R preys on P and O — not on O and N. Hence A, B and D are correct.

Question 9

2 marks

The diagram below shows a pendulum in motion, swinging from X to Z (passing through the lowest point Y).

Which of the following graphs shows the correct change in the potential energy (PE) of the ball as it swings from X to Z?

(1)

(2)

(3)

(4)

Answer

Option 4. Potential energy depends on height. The ball is highest at X and Z and lowest at Y, so PE is high at X, falls to a minimum at Y, then rises again to a high value at Z — a V-shaped graph. Graph (4) shows this. Graph (3) is inverted (PE highest at Y), and graphs (1) and (2) show a one-way change, none of which matches the swing.

Section B: Open-Ended Questions

Question 10

5 marks

Joe observed five living organisms J, K, L, M and N and made the following observations:

  • J and L are both plant-eaters.
  • When there is not enough light, M will not be able to produce oxygen.
  • When the population of L increases, the population of K will increase but the population of N will decrease.
(a) Organism J is shown in the food web below. Fill in the blanks with the correct living organisms K, L, M and N to complete the food web. (2m)
(b) Which organism in the above food web is both a prey and a predator? (1m)
(c) Joe then observed the living organisms in his school pond. After a few months, the surface of the pond was completely covered with plant B. How would this affect the population of Animal A? Explain your answer. (2m)

Answer

(a) M is the producer (it makes oxygen in light). The completed food web is:

M is the producer; J and L both feed on M (both are plant-eaters). N feeds on J, and K feeds on both N and L. When L increases, K (which eats L) increases, and K then preys more heavily on N, so N decreases — matching the clues.

(b) N. N is a predator (it feeds on J) and is also a prey (it is eaten by K), so it is both a prey and a predator.
(c) The population of Animal A will decrease. When Plant B completely covers the surface of the pond, no sunlight can pass through the water to reach the underwater Plant C. Plant C cannot photosynthesise and so stops giving out oxygen. Animal A will have less dissolved oxygen in the water and will eventually die, so its population decreases.

Question 11

3 marks

Ali placed some water plants into a beaker of muddy water A as shown below. He switched on the torch (lamp) and measured the time taken for the first bubble to appear on the surface of the water.

Ali repeated the experiment with two other types of muddy water, B and C. The table shows the conditions of the muddy water (least muddy → most muddy):

least muddymost muddy
CAB
(a) In which water, A, B or C, will the first bubble take the shortest time to appear on the surface of the water? Explain your answer. (2m)
(b) Without changing the apparatus, what can Ali do to shorten the amount of time for bubbles to first appear on the surface of the water? (1m)

Answer

(a) Water C. C is the least muddy, so it allows the most light to pass through to the water plant. The water plant therefore has the highest rate of photosynthesis and produces oxygen (the bubbles) the fastest, so the first bubble appears in the shortest time.
(b) Move the lamp (torch) nearer to the beaker. This increases the amount of light reaching the water plant, raising its rate of photosynthesis so that bubbles appear sooner.

Question 12

4 marks

The diagram below shows how a burner placed below a balloon causes the hot air balloon to rise into the sky.

(a) Complete the energy conversion that takes place in the hot air balloon when the burner is lit. (1m)
(b) Using the burner, what can be done to make the hot air balloon rise up higher in the sky? (1m)
(c) Explain your answer for part (b), using energy conversion. (2m)

Answer

(a) The completed energy conversion chain is:

Heat energy of the lit burner → Heat energy of the hot air in the balloon → Kinetic energy of the moving air in the balloon.

(b) Increase the flame of the burner (make the flame bigger / hotter). This makes the hot air balloon rise up higher in the sky.
(c) When the temperature of the flame increases, the air in the balloon gains more heat energy, which is converted into more kinetic energy of the moving air in the balloon. The greater kinetic energy of the rising hot air makes the balloon rise higher in the sky.
